title: Tuberculosis
authors: Mario C. B. Raviglione, Andrea Gori

items.title: Understanding the Global Impact of Tuberculosis
items.content: Tuberculosis (TB), caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, is a significant global health concern. It has returned to being the top cause of infectious death from a single agent in 2023.
keyPoints: TB is one of the oldest diseases affecting humans.
In 2023, TB became the leading cause of infectious death from a single agent.
Mycobacterium tuberculosis emerged ~70,000 years ago in Africa.

items.title: Transmission and Prevention of Tuberculosis
items.content: TB is primarily transmitted through airborne droplet nuclei from infectious pulmonary TB patients. Pharmacologic prophylaxis can prevent disease development in those infected.
keyPoints: Transmission occurs via airborne spread of droplet nuclei.
Pharmacologic prophylaxis prevents disease development post-infection.

items.title: Characteristics and Genetic Diversity of Mycobacterium tuberculosis
items.content: M. tuberculosis is a rod-shaped, acid-fast bacterium with substantial genetic variability, allowing for strain differentiation crucial in studying transmission dynamics.
keyPoints: M. tuberculosis measures 0.5 µm by 3 µm and is acid-fast due to mycolic acids.
Genome comprises 4.4 million base pairs and 4043 genes.
Genetic variability aids in distinguishing strains for transmission studies.

items.title: Global TB Burden and Distribution
items.content: In 2023, the WHO reported approximately 8.2 million new cases of tuberculosis (TB), with a significant majority occurring in low- and middle-income countries. However, these figures likely underestimate the true burden due to insufficient case detection.
keyPoints: 8.2 million new TB cases reported globally in 2023
97% of cases from low- and middle-income countries
Estimated total incident cases: 10.8 million (range, 10.1–11.7 million)
Two-thirds of global cases concentrated in eight countries: India, Indonesia, China, Philippines, Pakistan, Nigeria, Bangladesh, Democratic Republic of the Congo

items.title: TB Incidence and Mortality Trends
items.content: While TB incidence has stabilized or declined in high-income countries, it remains a significant issue globally. In 2023, an estimated 1.25 million deaths from TB occurred, with the majority in low- and middle-income regions.
keyPoints: 1.25 million estimated TB-related deaths in 2023
160,000 deaths among persons with HIV co-infection
98% of TB deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries
TB incidence decreasing in Africa but increasing in the Americas and Western Pacific

items.title: Drug Resistance in Tuberculosis
items.content: In 2023, a significant proportion of TB cases were drug-resistant. Multidrug-resistant (MDR) and rifampin-resistant (RR) TB remain major challenges due to limited diagnostic capacity.
keyPoints: 400,000 estimated cases of RR-TB and MDR-TB in 2023
16% of all new and previously treated TB cases were MDR/RR-TB
189,000 MDR/RR-TB cases enrolled on treatment due to limited DST capacity
Highest proportions of MDR/RR-TB among new cases in countries of the former Soviet Union

items.title: Transmission Dynamics and Infectiousness of Tuberculosis
items.content: M. tuberculosis is primarily transmitted via droplet nuclei from infectious pulmonary TB patients, with the smallest particles remaining airborne for hours. The most infectious cases are those with cavitary pulmonary disease or laryngeal TB, producing sputum with $10^{5}-10^{7}$ AFB/mL. Factors influencing transmission include contact intensity and environment, while HIV co-infection may reduce infectiousness.
keyPoints: Transmission occurs through droplet nuclei from coughing/sneezing.
Smallest particles (<5–10 μm) can remain airborne for hours.
Most infectious cases: cavitary pulmonary or laryngeal TB with $10^{5}-10^{7}$ AFB/mL in sputum.
Sputum smear-negative/culture-positive patients are less infectious but still responsible for up to 20% of transmission in some US studies.
Culture-negative pulmonary and extrapulmonary TB cases are essentially noninfectious.
HIV co-infection may reduce infectiousness due to fewer cavitations.
Crowding and poor ventilation increase transmission risk.
$R_{0}$ for TB varies widely: 0.24 in the Netherlands (1933–2007) to 4.3 in China (2012).

items.title: Factors Influencing Tuberculosis Infection Risk and Transmission
items.content: The risk of TB transmission is influenced by both exogenous factors, such as contact with infectious cases in crowded environments, and endogenous factors like immune competence. HIV-infected individuals or those on immunosuppressive therapy are at higher risk for acquiring TB infection.
keyPoints: Exogenous factors: contact probability, intimacy/duration of contact, case infectiousness, shared environment.
Endogenous factors: degree of immune competence.
Higher risk groups: HIV-infected patients, cancer treatment recipients, immunosuppressed individuals.
$R_{0}$ varies with environmental and social conditions.

items.title: Risk Factors and Timing of TB Disease Development Post-Infection
items.content: The progression from M. tuberculosis infection to active disease is influenced by endogenous factors such as immune system function, with primary TB being more common in children and immunocompromised individuals. Secondary TB, which occurs when the infection reactivates later in life, has a higher transmissibility due to cavitation. The risk of developing active TB within 18 months post-infection is significant, especially among HIV-infected persons.
keyPoints: Up to 10% of infected individuals will develop active TB over their lifetime, with half occurring within the first 18 months.
Primary TB is common in children and immunocompromised individuals but has low transmissibility.
Secondary TB is more infectious due to cavitation.
HIV co-infection significantly increases the risk of developing active TB.

items.title: Impact of HIV Co-Infection on TB Disease Progression
items.content: HIV co-infection is a potent risk factor for the development of active TB, as it suppresses cellular immunity. The likelihood of progression to active disease increases with greater immunosuppression, evidenced by lower CD4+ T cell counts in people living with HIV.
keyPoints: HIV co-infection dramatically raises the risk of active TB due to suppressed cellular immunity.
Risk varies from 2.6 to 13.3 cases per 100 person-years among TST-positive PLWH, increasing as CD4+ T cell count decreases.

items.subtopic: NATURAL HISTORY OF DISEASE | INFECTION AND MACROPHAGE INVASION
items.title: Natural History of Untreated Tuberculosis: Mortality Rates and Spontaneous Remission
items.content: Untreated tuberculosis (TB) has a high mortality rate, with significant differences between smear-positive and smear-negative cases. Historical data indicate that without treatment, about one-third of patients die within the first year post-diagnosis. For sputum smear-positive cases, 55% die within five years, and up to 86% (weighted mean, 70%) within ten years. Untreated paucibacillary smear-negative cases have a lower case fatality rate of around 20% at five years. Among survivors at five years, approximately 60% experience spontaneous remission.
keyPoints: One-third of untreated TB patients die within 1 year after diagnosis.
55% of sputum smear-positive cases are dead within 5 years; up to 86% (weighted mean, 70%) within 10 years.
Untreated paucibacillary smear-negative cases have a ~20% case fatality rate at 5 years.
~60% of survivors at 5 years undergo spontaneous remission.

items.subtopic: NATURAL HISTORY OF DISEASE | INFECTION AND MACROPHAGE INVASION
items.title: Mechanisms of M. tuberculosis Survival in Macrophages
items.content: Mycobacterium tuberculosis (M. tuberculosis) employs several mechanisms to survive within macrophages, primarily by inhibiting phagosome maturation and evading host defenses. Upon inhalation, a fraction (<10%) of bacilli reach the alveoli where they are initially taken up by myeloid dendritic cells and then by prototypic alternatively activated alveolar macrophages. M. tuberculosis binds to various macrophage receptors, including complement receptors and mannose receptors, facilitating phagocytosis. The bacterium inhibits phagosome–lysosome fusion through reduced acidification of the phagosome and interference with calcium signaling pathways. Lipoarabinomannan disrupts Ca²⁺/calmodulin-dependent processes, while blocking phosphatidylinositol 3-phosphate production impedes autophagy. The ESX-1 secretion system mediates macrophage rupture and subsequent infection spread.
keyPoints: <10% of inhaled M. tuberculosis bacilli reach the alveoli.
M. tuberculosis binds to receptors like complement receptors, mannose receptor, and Fcγ receptor on macrophages.
Survival depends on reduced phagosome acidification due to incomplete vesicular proton-adenosine triphosphatase assembly.
Lipoarabinomannan inhibits Ca²⁺/calmodulin pathway, impairing phagosome–lysosome fusion.
Bacterial factors block autophagy by preventing phosphatidylinositol 3-phosphate production.
ESX-1 secretion system mediates macrophage rupture and infection spread.
items.reference: Tuberculosis · NATURAL HISTORY OF DISEASE | INFECTION AND MACROPHAGE INVASION

items.title: Role of the katG Gene in M. tuberculosis Virulence
items.content: The katG gene is crucial for M. tuberculosis virulence as it encodes a catalase/peroxidase enzyme that protects against oxidative stress and activates isoniazid, enhancing bactericidal activity.
keyPoints: katG gene encodes catalase/peroxidase essential for protection against oxidative stress
Required for activation of isoniazid, leading to increased bactericidal activity

items.title: Importance of RD1 Locus in M. tuberculosis Pathogenicity
items.content: The RD1 locus is critical for virulence, encoding antigens ESAT-6 and CFP-10, and a secretion apparatus facilitating their egress; its absence in BCG strain attenuates virulence.
keyPoints: RD1 locus encodes 9.5-kb region with key antigens ESAT-6 (6 kDa) and CFP-10
Lack of RD1 is a major attenuation mutation in M. bovis BCG vaccine strain

items.title: Genetic Factors in Resistance to Tuberculosis
items.content: Genetic factors significantly influence innate resistance to M. tuberculosis infection and disease development, as evidenced by varying susceptibility across populations.
keyPoints: Resistance is polygenic, involving multiple genetic factors.
Differing degrees of TB susceptibility among different populations suggest a genetic basis for resistance.
Negative results in TST and IFN-γ release assays may accompany pathogen elimination due to innate resistance mechanisms.

items.title: Role of Specific Genes in Tuberculosis Susceptibility
items.content: Specific genes, such as Nramp1 in mice and its human homologue NRAMP1, play crucial roles in determining susceptibility to TB by regulating resistance mechanisms.
keyPoints: Nramp1 gene in mice regulates resistance/susceptibility to mycobacteria.
Human homologue NRAMP1 maps to chromosome 2q and may influence TB susceptibility, as suggested by studies among West Africans.

Polymorphisms in genes like major histocompatibility complex alleles, IFN-γ, T cell growth factor β, IL-10, mannose-binding protein, IFN-γ receptor, Toll-like receptor 2, vitamin D receptor, and IL-1 are associated with TB susceptibility.

items.subtopic: THE HOST RESPONSE, GRANULOMA FORMATION, AND “LATENCY”
items.title: Mechanisms of Granuloma Formation and Early Mycobacterial Growth
items.content: M. tuberculosis initiates infection by disseminating through lymph vessels to various organs, growing within macrophages before a cell-mediated immune response develops. The mycobacterium uses virulence mechanisms such as the secretion of ESAT-6 protein, which induces MMP9 from epithelial cells, promoting granuloma maturation and bacterial growth.
keyPoints: M. tuberculosis spreads through lymph vessels to lungs and other organs early in infection.
ESAT-6 protein induces secretion of matrix metalloproteinase 9 (MMP9), aiding granuloma formation.
Disruption of MMP9 function reduces bacterial growth.
items.reference: Tuberculosis · THE HOST RESPONSE, GRANULOMA FORMATION, AND “LATENCY”

items.subtopic: THE HOST RESPONSE, GRANULOMA FORMATION, AND “LATENCY”
items.title: Host Responses and Granulomatous Lesion Formation
items.content: About 2–4 weeks post-infection, two key host responses develop: a macrophage-activating cell-mediated immune response that kills bacilli, and a tissue-damaging delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction. The balance between these responses determines the progression of TB; granulomatous lesions form from activated macrophages and lymphocytes.
keyPoints: Macrophage-activating CMI response occurs 2–4 weeks after infection.
Tissue-damaging response results in caseous necrosis and limits mycobacterial growth.
Balance between immune responses determines TB progression.
items.reference: Tuberculosis · THE HOST RESPONSE, GRANULOMA FORMATION, AND “LATENCY”

items.subtopic: THE HOST RESPONSE, GRANULOMA FORMATION, AND “LATENCY”
items.title: Latency vs. Persistence of M. tuberculosis
items.content: The term 'latent TB infection' (LTBI) describes a state with no active disease despite immune response to M. tuberculosis antigens. However, bacilli may remain active in biofilms within necrotic areas, challenging the notion of dormancy and suggesting 'persistence' as a more accurate descriptor.
keyPoints: 'Latent TB infection' indicates persistent immune response without active disease.
Bacilli may form biofilms in necrotic areas during latency, remaining active.
The term 'persister' better describes the behavior of bacilli than 'latent'.
items.reference: Tuberculosis · THE HOST RESPONSE, GRANULOMA FORMATION, AND “LATENCY”

items.subtopic: MACROPHAGE-ACTIVATING RESPONSE | DELAYED-TYPE HYPERSENSITIVITY | ROLE OF MACROPHAGES AND MONOCYTES
items.title: Role of Macrophages in Containing Tuberculosis Infection
items.content: Macrophages play a crucial role in the early containment of tuberculosis (TB) by activating cell-mediated immunity. Activated macrophages aggregate around lesions, neutralizing tubercle bacilli without causing extensive tissue damage. However, if the macrophage-activating response is weak, delayed hypersensitivity reactions may lead to lung tissue destruction and cavity formation.
keyPoints: Activated macrophages are essential for containing TB infection by aggregating around lesions and neutralizing tubercle bacilli.
Caseous necrosis in the center of lesions resembles soft cheese and can persist with viable bacilli even after healing.
Weak macrophage-activating response leads to intensified delayed hypersensitivity reactions, causing lung tissue destruction and cavity formation.
items.reference: Tuberculosis · MACROPHAGE-ACTIVATING RESPONSE | DELAYED-TYPE HYPERSENSITIVITY | ROLE OF MACROPHAGES AND MONOCYTES

items.subtopic: MACROPHAGE-ACTIVATING RESPONSE | DELAYED-TYPE HYPERSENSITIVITY | ROLE OF MACROPHAGES AND MONOCYTES
items.title: Cell-Mediated Immunity in Tuberculosis Defense
items.content: Cell-mediated immunity, primarily involving macrophages and T cells (especially CD4+ T lymphocytes), is critical for defense against M. tuberculosis. Macrophages phagocytose bacilli and produce cytokines like IFN-γ, while T cells induce protection through cytokine production. However, alternatively activated alveolar macrophages may be susceptible to early TB growth due to limited proinflammatory activity.
keyPoints: Cell-mediated immunity involves macrophages phagocytosing tubercle bacilli and T cells producing cytokines like IFN-γ.
CD4+ T lymphocytes are mainly responsible for inducing protection, though CD8+ T cells also play a role.
Alternatively activated alveolar macrophages have limited proinflammatory activity, making them susceptible to early TB growth.
items.reference: Tuberculosis · MACROPHAGE-ACTIVATING RESPONSE | DELAYED-TYPE HYPERSENSITIVITY | ROLE OF MACROPHAGES AND MONOCYTES

items.subtopic: ROLE OF T LYMPHOCYTES | MYCOBACTERIAL LIPIDS AND PROTEINS
items.title: Role of CD4+ and CD8+ T Cells in TB Defense
items.content: CD4+ T cells are crucial for defense against M. tuberculosis, as they differentiate into cytokine-producing TH1 or TH2 cells. TH1 cells produce IFN-γ and IL-2, activating macrophages, while TH2 cells may promote humoral immunity through IL-4, IL-5, IL-10, and IL-13 production. CD8+ T cells contribute via cytotoxic responses and cytokine production (IFN-γ and TNF-α).
keyPoints: CD4+ T cell defects explain HIV-associated TB susceptibility.
TH1 cells produce IFN-γ and IL-2; TH2 cells produce IL-4, IL-5, IL-10, and IL-13.
IFN-γ activates macrophages via reactive oxygen/nitrogen intermediates.
TNF-α is crucial for TB control but requires balanced activation.
CD8+ T cells have protective roles through cytotoxicity and cytokine production.
items.reference: Tuberculosis · ROLE OF T LYMPHOCYTES | MYCOBACTERIAL LIPIDS AND PROTEINS

items.subtopic: ROLE OF T LYMPHOCYTES | MYCOBACTERIAL LIPIDS AND PROTEINS
items.title: Mycobacterial Lipids and Proteins in Immune Recognition
items.content: Lipids, such as 19-kDa lipoprotein, are recognized by the innate immune system via Toll-like receptors on dendritic cells. Protein antigens from M. tuberculosis, particularly secreted ones like those of the antigen 85 complex (e.g., 30-kDa major secretory protein and ESAT-6), are crucial for eliciting T lymphocyte responses and protective immunity.
keyPoints: 19-kDa lipoprotein triggers innate immune recognition via Toll-like receptors.
Secreted mycobacterial proteins, like antigen 85 complex components, are vital for T cell response.
Protective immunity involves reactivity to multiple mycobacterial antigens.
Antigen 85 complex includes key proteins such as the 30-kDa major secretory protein and ESAT-6.
items.reference: Tuberculosis · ROLE OF T LYMPHOCYTES | MYCOBACTERIAL LIPIDS AND PROTEINS

items.title: Understanding TST Reactivity in Tuberculosis Diagnosis
items.content: The tuberculin skin test (TST) reactivity is based on delayed-type hypersensitivity to M. tuberculosis, primarily involving CD4+ T lymphocytes that proliferate and produce cytokines at the site of the test. While a positive TST indicates some level of immunity and reduced susceptibility to new infections, it does not guarantee protection against reactivation or reinfection with different strains.
keyPoints: TST is used for diagnosing M. tuberculosis infection in asymptomatic individuals.
CD4+ T lymphocytes play a crucial role in the cellular mechanisms of TST reactivity.
Positive TST results do not ensure full immunity; active TB can occur with strongly positive reactions.
Reinfection with new strains is possible even after treatment for active TB.

items.title: Prevalence and Patterns of Extrapulmonary Tuberculosis
items.content: Tuberculosis can manifest as pulmonary, extrapulmonary, or both. The occurrence of extrapulmonary TB is influenced by host immunologic status and bacterial strains, affecting 10–40% of patients. In HIV-infected individuals, up to two-thirds may present with either concurrent pulmonary and extrapulmonary TB or solely extrapulmonary TB.
keyPoints: Extrapulmonary TB occurs in 10–40% of all TB cases.
HIV-infected patients have a higher prevalence of both pulmonary and extrapulmonary TB, or exclusively extrapulmonary TB.

items.title: Primary Pulmonary TB: Presentation and Complications
items.content: Primary pulmonary TB occurs soon after initial infection, often asymptomatic or presenting with fever and pleuritic chest pain. It commonly involves the middle and lower lung zones due to air distribution patterns. The Ghon focus is a typical lesion that may heal spontaneously as a calcified nodule but can lead to complications such as pleural effusion in up to two-thirds of cases, especially in young children or immunocompromised individuals.
keyPoints: Primary TB often asymptomatic or presents with fever and pleuritic chest pain.
Ghon focus usually peripheral, may heal as calcified nodule.
Pleural effusion found in up to two-thirds of cases.
Complications include rapid progression to clinical illness in young children or immunocompromised individuals.

items.title: Progressive Primary TB: Pathophysiology and Risks
items.content: In cases of progressive primary TB, the initial lesion enlarges, undergoes necrosis, and cavitation develops. This is particularly concerning in young children and immunocompromised persons due to immature or impaired cell-mediated immunity. Enlarged lymph nodes can cause bronchial obstruction or rupture into airways, leading to pneumonia with potential for necrosis and cavitation.
keyPoints: Progressive primary TB involves lesion enlargement, necrosis, and cavitation.
High risk in young children and immunocompromised individuals.
Lymph node involvement can cause bronchial obstruction or rupture into airways.

items.title: Postprimary (Adult-Type) Pulmonary TB: Characteristics and Outcomes
items.content: Postprimary TB, often termed adult-type TB, typically affects the apical and posterior segments of the upper lobes due to higher oxygen tension. It can range from small infiltrates to extensive cavity disease. Untreated severe pulmonary TB may lead to rapid mortality ('galloping consumption'), while others may experience spontaneous remission or chronic debilitating progression. Treatment usually results in improvement within weeks.
keyPoints: Postprimary TB affects apical and posterior segments of upper lobes.
Ranges from small infiltrates to extensive cavity disease.
Untreated severe cases can lead to rapid mortality ('galloping consumption').
Treatment leads to defervescence, decreased cough, weight gain within weeks.

items.title: Understanding Hemoptysis in Pulmonary Tuberculosis
items.content: Hemoptysis is a significant symptom in pulmonary tuberculosis, occurring in 20-30% of cases. It can result from erosion of blood vessels within cavities or due to Rasmussen's aneurysm and aspergilloma formation.
keyPoints: Hemoptysis occurs in 20–30% of cases
Causes include vessel erosion, Rasmussen's aneurysm, and aspergilloma

items.title: Recognizing Systemic Features of Pulmonary Tuberculosis
items.content: Systemic features such as fever in up to 80% of cases and wasting are common. However, the absence of fever does not exclude tuberculosis. Common hematologic findings include mild anemia, leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein levels.
keyPoints: Fever occurs in up to 80% of cases
Common hematologic findings: mild anemia, leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, elevated ESR/CRP
Absence of fever does not exclude TB

items.title: Lymph Node TB: Common Presentation and Diagnostic Approach
items.content: Lymph node TB, or tuberculous lymphadenitis, is the most common form of extrapulmonary TB, especially prevalent in PLWH and children. It presents as painless swelling, often at posterior cervical and supraclavicular sites. Diagnosis is primarily established through fine-needle aspiration biopsy with a high yield, or surgical excision biopsy.
keyPoints: Most common extrapulmonary TB form (35% worldwide, >40% in the US).
Painless swelling of lymph nodes; scrofula historically noted.
Fine-needle aspiration biopsy has up to 80% diagnostic yield.
Cultures positive in 70–80% of cases.
Differential diagnosis includes lymphomas, metastatic carcinomas, Kikuchi disease.

items.title: Pleural TB: Clinical Features and Diagnostic Strategies
items.content: Pleural TB accounts for ~20% of extrapulmonary cases, often presenting with pleural effusion due to hypersensitivity or contiguous spread. Diagnosis involves thoracentesis showing exudative fluid characteristics; needle biopsy is preferred over fluid analysis, revealing granulomas or positive cultures in up to 80% of cases.
keyPoints: ~20% of extrapulmonary TB cases involve the pleura.
Effusion: straw-colored, protein >50% serum level (~4–6 g/dL), low glucose, pH ~7.3.
AFBs rarely seen; cultures often false negative.
Needle biopsy recommended; reveals granulomas or positive culture in up to 80%.
Real-time nucleic acid amplification (Xpert MTB/RIF) yields positive results in ~75%.

items.title: Tuberculous Empyema: Recognition and Management
items.content: Tuberculous empyema is a rare complication of pulmonary TB, resulting from cavity rupture into the pleural space. It may lead to bronchopleural fistula formation. Prompt chemotherapy response is noted, with glucocorticoids potentially reducing symptom duration.
keyPoints: Less common complication of pulmonary TB.
Results from cavity rupture and organism spillage.
May create bronchopleural fistula.
Responds rapidly to chemotherapy.
Glucocorticoids may reduce fever/chest pain duration.

items.title: Tuberculous Empyema: When Surgical Intervention is Necessary
items.content: In tuberculous empyema, surgical drainage and possibly decortication are required to manage severe pleural fibrosis and restrictive lung disease. This intervention improves lung function when conservative measures fail.
keyPoints: Tuberculous empyema may lead to severe pleural fibrosis and restrictive lung disease.
Surgical drainage is often necessary as an adjunct to chemotherapy.
Decortication of the thickened visceral pleura may be required for improved lung function.

items.title: Genitourinary Tuberculosis: Recognizing and Diagnosing
items.content: Genitourinary tuberculosis, accounting for ~10–15% of extrapulmonary cases, often presents with nonspecific symptoms. Diagnosis is confirmed by culture of urine specimens or biopsy in female patients.
keyPoints: Accounts for ~10–15% of all extrapulmonary TB cases.
Symptoms include those of urinary tract infection: frequency, dysuria, nocturia, hematuria, and abdominal or flank pain.
Up to 75% have CXR abnormalities suggesting pulmonary disease.
Urinalysis abnormal in 90%, revealing pyuria and hematuria.
Culture of three morning urine specimens yields diagnosis in nearly 90% of cases.

items.title: Skeletal Tuberculosis: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Skeletal tuberculosis, responsible for ~10% of extrapulmonary cases in the U.S., often affects weight-bearing joints. Diagnosis involves imaging and biopsy; severe cases may require surgical intervention.
keyPoints: Responsible for ~10% of extrapulmonary TB cases in the United States.
Commonly affects weight-bearing joints: spine (40%), hips (13%), knees (10%).
Spinal TB often involves two or more adjacent vertebral bodies, leading to kyphosis and paravertebral abscesses.
Diagnosis requires imaging (CT/MRI) and biopsy; cultures are usually positive.
Severe cases may necessitate surgery in addition to chemotherapy.

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Tuberculous Meningitis
items.content: Tuberculous meningitis is a severe form of extrapulmonary tuberculosis, often presenting subtly with headache and mental changes. Diagnosis relies on CSF analysis showing high leukocyte counts, elevated protein, and low glucose levels. The Xpert MTB/RIF assay is preferred for initial diagnosis due to its sensitivity.
keyPoints: Tuberculous meningitis accounts for ~5% of extrapulmonary TB cases in the U.S.
CSF analysis reveals high leukocyte count (up to 1000/μL), protein content of 1–8 g/L, and low glucose concentration
Xpert MTB/RIF assay has a sensitivity of up to 80% and is preferred for initial diagnosis
Treatment should be initiated immediately upon a positive Xpert MTB/RIF result
Adjunctive glucocorticoids may enhance survival in patients >14 years but do not reduce neurologic sequelae

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Prognosis of Tuberculous Meningitis
items.content: Tuberculous meningitis often presents with headache, mental changes, and a prodrome of fever and malaise. If untreated, it progresses to severe neurological symptoms. Early diagnosis is crucial as the disease responds to chemotherapy but may result in neurologic sequelae in 25% of cases.
keyPoints: Initial presentation includes headache, slight mental changes, low-grade fever, malaise, anorexia, and irritability
Disease evolves over 1–2 weeks, longer than typical bacterial meningitis
Paresis of cranial nerves and focal ischemia due to cerebral artery involvement are common
If unrecognized, tuberculous meningitis is uniformly fatal
Neurologic sequelae occur in 25% of treated cases

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Tuberculoma
items.content: Tuberculomas are space-occupying lesions in the CNS, presenting with seizures and focal signs. Diagnosis requires biopsy, while imaging shows contrast-enhanced ring lesions.
keyPoints: Tuberculomas present as one or more space-occupying lesions causing seizures and focal signs
CT or MRI reveals contrast-enhanced ring lesions
Biopsy is necessary to establish the diagnosis

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Tuberculous Peritonitis
items.content: Tuberculous peritonitis is suspected in patients with nonspecific abdominal pain, fever, and ascites. Diagnosis can be challenging due to overlapping symptoms with other conditions like cirrhosis. Ascitic fluid analysis typically shows an exudative nature with high protein content and lymphocytic predominance. Direct smear and culture have low yields; thus, peritoneal biopsy is often required.
keyPoints: Suspicion arises from nonspecific abdominal pain, fever, and ascites.
Ascitic fluid: Exudative, high protein, leukocytosis (lymphocytic predominance).
Low yield of direct smear and culture; peritoneal biopsy often needed.

items.title: Recognizing and Treating Tuberculous Pericarditis
items.content: Tuberculous pericarditis can present subacutely or acutely with symptoms like dyspnea, fever, retrosternal pain, and a friction rub. Echocardiography may show effusion and pericardial thickening. Diagnosis is confirmed by pericardiocentesis with lymphocyte-rich exudative fluid analysis. Treatment is crucial as untreated cases are fatal; glucocorticoid therapy's role remains debated.
keyPoints: Symptoms: Dyspnea, fever, retrosternal pain, friction rub.
Echocardiography shows effusion and pericardial thickening.
Pericardial fluid: Exudative, lymphocyte-rich; culture positive in up to two-thirds.
High case fatality rates (~40%); glucocorticoid therapy's benefit is debated.

items.title: Identifying Miliary Tuberculosis
items.content: Miliary tuberculosis results from hematogenous spread, presenting with nonspecific symptoms like fever and weight loss. It can manifest in various organs, leading to hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy. Choroidal tubercles are pathognomonic in up to 30% of cases. Diagnosis requires high suspicion, often supported by a miliary reticulonodular pattern on chest X-ray.
keyPoints: Caused by hematogenous spread; granulomas resemble millet seeds.
Symptoms: Fever, night sweats, anorexia, weight loss.
Physical findings: Hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, lymphadenopathy.
Choroidal tubercles pathognomonic in up to 30% of cases.

items.title: Recognizing Miliary Tuberculosis: A Critical Early Diagnosis
items.content: Miliary tuberculosis, if unrecognized, is lethal but can be cured with proper early treatment. It often presents without radiographic abnormalities initially, especially in PLWH.
keyPoints: No radiographic abnormality may be evident early, particularly among PLWH.
Sputum-smear microscopy is negative in most cases.
Bronchoalveolar lavage and transbronchial biopsy are more likely to provide bacteriologic confirmation.
Proper early treatment can cure miliary TB.

items.title: Understanding Cryptic Military Tuberculosis in the Elderly
items.content: Cryptic military tuberculosis is a rare, chronic form seen in the elderly characterized by mild intermittent fever and anemia, leading to meningeal involvement before death.
keyPoints: Characterized by mild intermittent fever and anemia.
Ultimate progression leads to meningeal involvement preceding death.
Rare presentation typically observed in the elderly.

items.title: Post-TB Lung Disease: Recognizing Aspergillomas and Chronic Pulmonary Aspergillosis
items.content: A significant number of TB survivors experience chronic lung impairment, including aspergillomas and chronic pulmonary aspergillosis. These conditions can mimic TB recurrence.
keyPoints: Chronic impairment includes dyspnea, residual cough, bronchiectasis, and pulmonary hypertension.
Aspergillomas may colonize large residual cavities, causing respiratory symptoms.
Detection of Aspergillus precipitins (IgG) suggests chronic pulmonary aspergillosis.
Triazoles for ≥6 months show improvement or stabilization in 60–75% of cases.

items.title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of Extrapulmonary TB in PLWH
items.content: Extrapulmonary TB is prevalent among people living with HIV, often presenting without cavitation. The Xpert MTB/RIF assay should be the initial diagnostic test due to its high sensitivity (>80%) and specificity (98%). A positive result warrants immediate treatment initiation because delays can be fatal. However, a negative result does not exclude TB; culture remains the gold standard.
keyPoints: Extrapulmonary TB is common in PLWH, documented in 40–60% of cases.
Xpert MTB/RIF assay has >80% sensitivity and 98% specificity for pulmonary TB diagnosis.
A negative Xpert result does not exclude TB; culture remains the gold standard.

items.title: Understanding IRIS in PLWH with TB
items.content: IRIS, or TB immune reconstitution disease, occurs in ~10% of HIV-infected TB patients after ART initiation. It is characterized by exacerbations in systemic and respiratory signs due to improved immune response. The risk of IRIS increases with earlier ART initiation post-TB treatment and lower baseline CD4+ T-cell counts.
keyPoints: IRIS occurs in ~10% of HIV-infected TB patients after ART initiation.
More common among those with advanced immunosuppression and extrapulmonary TB.
Higher risk when ART is started early post-TB treatment and baseline CD4+ T-cell count is low.

items.title: Management of IRIS in PLWH with CNS TB
items.content: IRIS can lead to severe complications, especially in CNS TB. ART should be delayed for the first 8 weeks during TB treatment in these patients. For severe paradoxical reactions, glucocorticoids like prednisolone (1.5 mg/kg per day initially) are recommended to reduce hospitalization needs and alleviate symptoms.
keyPoints: ART initiation is deferred for 8 weeks in CNS TB cases due to IRIS risk.
Glucocorticoids used for severe paradoxical reactions: prednisolone (1.5 mg/kg per day initially).
Glucocorticoids reduce proinflammatory cytokines, improving symptoms and outcomes.

items.title: High-Index of Suspicion for Early TB Diagnosis in High-Risk Populations
items.content: Early diagnosis of tuberculosis (TB) is crucial and relies on maintaining a high index of suspicion, especially in high-risk populations. These individuals often present with typical symptoms and characteristic chest radiograph findings such as upper-lobe infiltrates with cavities.
keyPoints: High-risk groups include those with typical symptoms and classic chest radiographs showing upper-lobe infiltrates with cavities
Diagnosis can be missed in elderly nursing-home residents or teenagers with focal infiltrates

items.title: Diagnostic Approaches and Laboratory Network for TB
items.content: Effective diagnosis of TB requires a well-organized microbiology laboratory network that distributes tasks across different levels of the healthcare system. At secondary levels, real-time automated nucleic acid amplification technology like Xpert MTB/RIF assay is used, while traditional AFB microscopy remains an option where newer tools are unavailable.
keyPoints: Well-organized microbiology lab network essential
Secondary level diagnosis: use of Xpert MTB/RIF for rapid detection and drug resistance
Tertiary level involves molecular tests, rapid culture, and DST

items.title: Why Xpert MTB/RIF is the Preferred First-Line Diagnostic Test for TB
items.content: The Xpert MTB/RIF assay is favored as a first-line diagnostic test due to its rapid detection capabilities, high specificity and sensitivity, and minimal biosafety requirements. It can simultaneously detect TB and rifampin resistance in under 2 hours.
keyPoints: High specificity (98%) and sensitivity (85% overall, reaching 98% among AFB-positive cases).
Simultaneous detection of TB and rifampin resistance with a sensitivity of 96% for rifampin resistance.
WHO recommends its use worldwide as the first-line diagnostic test in all adults and children with signs or symptoms of active TB.

items.title: How Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra Improves Sensitivity for Smear-Negative Cases
items.content: The Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra assay enhances sensitivity, especially in smear-negative and culture-positive cases, by including 'trace calls' as positive results. This adjustment is crucial for improving diagnosis among high-risk groups like PLWH.
keyPoints: Overall sensitivity of 90%, increasing to 107% with 'trace calls', but 86% without them.
Specificity decreases to 96% when including 'trace calls' but returns to 98% if excluded.
Sensitivity for PLWH is 88% and specificity 95%; rifampin resistance detection sensitivity is 94%.

items.title: When to Use Xpert MTB/RIF or Ultra in Extrapulmonary TB Diagnosis
items.content: Xpert MTB/RIF and Ultra should be the initial tests for diagnosing extrapulmonary TB, particularly in CSF from suspected TB meningitis cases. Sensitivity varies by specimen type, with synovial fluid and lymph node biopsy showing high sensitivity.
keyPoints: Initial test choice for extrapulmonary TB diagnosis, especially in CSF for TB meningitis suspicion.
Sensitivity ranges: 50% in pleural fluid (Xpert MTB/RIF) to 100% with Ultra in lymph node biopsy.
'Trace calls' should be considered true positives in high-risk groups like PLWH and children.

items.title: Why Use LED Fluorescence Microscopy for AFB Detection?
items.content: LED fluorescence microscopy is recommended over traditional methods due to its higher sensitivity and cost-effectiveness. It is particularly beneficial in low-resource settings where conventional light sources are impractical.
keyPoints: AFB microscopy has 40–60% sensitivity in culture-confirmed pulmonary TB cases.
Auramine-rhodamine staining with fluorescence microscopy is more sensitive than Ziehl-Neelsen method.
WHO recommends LED fluorescence microscopes as they are cost-effective and highly sensitive.

items.title: How Long Does Mycobacterial Culture Take for TB Diagnosis?
items.content: Mycobacterial culture, particularly using the MGIT system, can confirm TB within 2–3 weeks. This is faster than traditional methods due to advanced technologies like liquid cultures and molecular identification.
keyPoints: MGIT cultures become positive in 10 days to 2–3 weeks.
Conventional media require 4–8 weeks for growth detection.
Modern labs use liquid culture and molecular methods, reducing confirmation time to 2–3 weeks.

items.title: Why Universal Drug Susceptibility Testing (DST) is Crucial in TB Management?
items.content: Universal DST, as recommended by WHO, involves testing for rifampin resistance to identify MDR-TB. Expanded rapid susceptibility testing for isoniazid and key second-line drugs is mandatory when RR-TB is detected.
keyPoints: WHO recommends universal DST for all TB patients.
DST should include at least rifampin for initial isolates of M. tuberculosis.
Rifampin resistance serves as a proxy for MDR-TB diagnosis.

items.title: How Rapid Molecular Techniques Enhance TB Drug Susceptibility Testing?
items.content: Molecular techniques provide rapid results, with direct susceptibility testing on liquid medium offering an average reporting time of 3 weeks. Genotypic methods identify mutations associated with resistance to rifampin and isoniazid.
keyPoints: Direct molecular techniques can yield results in approximately 3 weeks.
Indirect testing on solid medium may take ≥8 weeks.
Genotypic methods detect mutations in rpoB, katG, and inhA for rapid identification of resistance.

items.title: Why Line Probe Assays (LPAs) are Recommended by WHO for TB Drug Resistance Detection?
items.content: WHO recommends LPAs for detecting isoniazid and rifampin resistance in sputum smear-positive specimens or cultured isolates. They also recommend second-line LPAs for rapid detection of fluoroquinolone and injectable drug resistance in RR-TB or MDR-TB.
keyPoints: WHO recommends LPAs for detecting isoniazid and rifampin resistance.
Second-line LPAs are recommended for fluoroquinolones and second-line injectables in confirmed RR-TB or MDR-TB cases.
LPAs do not eliminate the need for conventional phenotypic DST to identify other drug resistances.

items.title: Why CXR is a Primary Tool for Suspected Pulmonary TB
items.content: CXR (chest x-ray) is utilized as an initial imaging technique due to its high sensitivity in detecting pulmonary tuberculosis (TB), despite having poor specificity. It helps establish the initial suspicion of TB based on abnormal findings, especially when patients present with respiratory symptoms.
keyPoints: CXR has high sensitivity but poor specificity for pulmonary TB.
Typical patterns include upper-lobe disease with infiltrates and cavities.
No radiographic pattern is pathognomonic in the era of HIV/AIDS.
Digital CXR offers advantages like reduced procedure time, lower costs, superior quality imaging, and telemedicine assistance.

items.title: When to Use CT or MRI for TB Diagnosis
items.content: CT scans are useful for interpreting ambiguous findings on CXR and diagnosing extrapulmonary TB forms, such as intrabdominal disease and Pott's disease. MRI is particularly helpful in diagnosing bone lesions and intracranial TB.
keyPoints: CT helps interpret questionable CXR findings and diagnose extrapulmonary TB (e.g., intrabdominal disease, Pott's disease).
MRI is useful for diagnosing bone lesions and intracranial TB.

items.title: Use of Sputum Induction in Pulmonary TB Diagnosis
items.content: Sputum induction by ultrasonic nebulization of hypertonic saline is useful for patients unable to spontaneously produce sputum. This method can aid in diagnosing pulmonary TB, especially when radiographic abnormalities suggest other diagnoses.
keyPoints: Useful for patients who cannot produce spontaneous sputum
Induced specimens should be tested with Xpert MTB/RIF assay, mycobacterial culture, and AFB smear

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Extrapulmonary TB
items.content: Invasive diagnostic procedures are indicated for suspected extrapulmonary TB. Testing of specimens from involved sites, such as CSF and pleural fluid, is essential. Biopsy and culture of bone marrow and liver tissue have good yields in disseminated TB, especially in PLWH.
keyPoints: Indicated for suspected extrapulmonary TB
Biopsy and culture of bone marrow and liver tissue are useful in disseminated TB

items.title: Xpert MTB/RIF Assay in HIV-Associated TB Diagnosis
items.content: The Xpert MTB/RIF assay is preferred for rapid diagnosis of TB in PLWH due to its simplicity and increased sensitivity. It has a sensitivity of ~60–70% among AFB-negative, culture-positive cases and 97–98% among AFB-positive cases.
keyPoints: Preferred rapid diagnostic test for PLWH
Sensitivity: ~60–70% in AFB-negative, culture-positive cases; 97–98% in AFB-positive cases

items.subtopic: BIOMARKERS | DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 1)
items.title: Limitations of Current TB Diagnostics and the Need for New Biomarkers
items.content: Current diagnostics for tuberculosis (TB) have limitations, particularly in high-prevalence settings. Research is focused on developing new biomarkers that could serve as point-of-care tests to improve disease detection and triage.
keyPoints: WHO has defined target product profiles for TB biomarker research.
Host biomarkers like antibodies, cytokines, chemokines, and RNA signatures are promising but require further validation.
items.reference: Tuberculosis · BIOMARKERS | DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: BIOMARKERS | DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 1)
items.title: Comparing TB Skin Tests and IGRAs for TB Infection Diagnosis
items.content: Skin tests and Interferon-Gamma Release Assays (IGRAs) are the primary methods for diagnosing TB infection. Each has limitations, particularly in high TB/HIV prevalence settings.
keyPoints: TST measures skin response to tuberculin PPD but lacks specificity due to cross-reactivity with nontuberculous mycobacteria and BCG vaccination.
IGRAs measure IFN-γ release in response to ESAT-6 and CFP-10 antigens, offering higher specificity than TST.
items.reference: Tuberculosis · BIOMARKERS | DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: BIOMARKERS | DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 1)
items.title: Advantages of New TB Skin Tests and IGRAs
items.content: New TB skin tests using ESAT-6 and CFP-10 antigens, along with IGRAs, provide improved accuracy over traditional methods like TST.
keyPoints: TBSTs have accuracy similar to IGRAs and are useful for PLWH, children, and BCG-vaccinated persons.
IGRAs require specialized laboratory settings but offer logistical convenience and objective measurements.
items.reference: Tuberculosis · BIOMARKERS | DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 2) | Tuberculosis
items.title: Preferred Testing for TB Infection in Different Populations
items.content: In the United States, IGRA is preferred over TST for screening individuals over age 5 for TB infection, while TST remains preferred for children under 5 due to limited data on IGRAs. For high-risk groups like PLWH, both tests may be used to optimize sensitivity.
keyPoints: IGRA preferred over TST for persons >5 years in the US
TST preferred for children <5 years due to lack of IGRA data
Both tests can be used for high-risk individuals like PLWH
Two-step approach (TST followed by IGRA) recommended in Canada and some European countries
items.reference: Tuberculosis · DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 2) | Tuberculosis

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 2) | Tuberculosis
items.title: Limitations of TB Infection Tests
items.content: Both TST and IGRA have limitations in diagnosing TB infection, including low predictive value for progression to active disease, inability to differentiate between active TB and latent infection, and reduced sensitivity in immunocompromised patients.
keyPoints: Low predictive value for identifying high-risk individuals
Cannot distinguish active TB from latent TB infection
Inability to differentiate new infections from reinfections
Reduced sensitivity in immunocompromised patients
items.reference: Tuberculosis · DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 2) | Tuberculosis

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 2) | Tuberculosis
items.title: Considerations for IGRA Testing
items.content: IGRA testing may yield indeterminate results in patients with anti-IFN-$ γ $ autoantibodies due to its reliance on IFN-$ γ $ elaboration. Additionally, a TST administered too close to an IGRA can boost the IGRA response.
keyPoints: Indeterminate IGRA results possible with anti-IFN-$ γ $ autoantibodies
TST may boost IGRA response if interval >3 days
items.reference: Tuberculosis · DIAGNOSIS OF M. TUBERCULOSIS INFECTION (part 2) | Tuberculosis

items.title: Standard TB Regimen: Importance of Daily Administration
items.content: The standard regimen for treating drug-susceptible TB consists of a 2-month intensive phase with isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol, followed by a 4-month continuation phase with isoniazid and rifampin. Daily administration is crucial to avoid treatment failure, relapse, and drug resistance.
keyPoints: 2HRZE/4HR regimen cures >90% of patients
Daily administration reduces risk of treatment failure and drug resistance
Intermittent regimens are associated with increased risks

items.title: Alternative 4-Month Regimen for Specific Populations
items.content: A 4-month regimen including isoniazid, pyrazinamide (first 2 months), rifapentine, and moxifloxacin is recommended by WHO and CDC as an alternative for patients aged 12 years or older, including those with HIV. This regimen should not be used in certain populations like children under 12 or pregnant women.
keyPoints: 4-month regimen: 2HPMZ/2HPM
Noninferior to standard 6-month regimen
Not recommended for patients <40 kg, <12 years, severe TB forms, low CD4+ count, pregnant or breastfeeding

items.title: Management of Cavitary Pulmonary TB with Delayed Sputum-Culture Conversion
items.content: Patients with cavitary pulmonary TB who remain culture-positive at 2 months should be retested for drug-resistant TB and consider a change in regimen. A full course of 6 months with four-drug therapy is recommended, avoiding interruptions longer than 4 weeks.
keyPoints: Retest for drug resistance if culture-positive at 2 months
Consider changing regimen
Full 6-month course without >4-week interruptions

items.title: Importance of Adherence in Tuberculosis Treatment
items.content: Poor adherence to tuberculosis treatment can lead to drug resistance, making it crucial for both patients and providers to ensure consistent medication intake. Factors affecting adherence include patient-related issues such as lack of belief in the illness's severity, concomitant medical conditions like substance abuse, social support deficits, stigma fears, and poverty. Provider-related factors encompass inadequate support and inconvenient clinical services.
keyPoints: Poor adherence is a major impediment to curing tuberculosis.
Inconsistent drug exposure can lead to drug-resistant tubercle bacilli.
Patient-related factors: lack of belief in illness severity, substance abuse, lack of social support, stigma fears, poverty.
Provider-related factors: inadequate patient support and inconvenient services.

items.title: Effective Interventions to Enhance Tuberculosis Treatment Adherence
items.content: To improve adherence rates, interventions should include social support packages that provide educational, psychological, and material assistance. Health education about the disease's seriousness and treatment importance is crucial from the start of therapy. Psychological support can help mitigate stigma-related issues, while material support (e.g., food or financial aid) reduces indirect costs and income loss impacts. The RATIONS trial demonstrated nutritional support's effectiveness in reducing TB mortality and incidence.
keyPoints: Social support interventions include educational, psychological, and material assistance.
Health education on disease seriousness and treatment adherence is essential.
Psychological support addresses stigma-related issues.
Material support reduces indirect costs and income loss impacts.
RATIONS trial: Nutritional support led to rapid weight gain, decreased mortality, and 39–48% reduction in TB incidence.

items.title: Optimizing TB Treatment Adherence
items.content: Directly observed therapy (DOT) is crucial for ensuring adherence to tuberculosis treatment due to the public health importance of TB and lack of tools to predict self-administered treatment adherence. Various DOT methods include in-person supervision, nondaily DOT, and video-observed therapy (VOT), with VOT being superior when technology allows.
keyPoints: Directly observed therapy (DOT) is crucial for ensuring adherence due to the public health importance of TB.
WHO, ATS, CDC, and IDSA recommend direct supervision during the initial phase of treatment.
Community- or home-based DOT is preferred over facility-based DOT or unsupervised treatment.
VOT has shown similar outcomes to in-person DOT and can replace it when Internet access and video communication technology are available.
Digital health tools, like digital medication monitors, facilitate adherence monitoring.

items.title: Benefits of Fixed-Dose Combination Products in TB Treatment
items.content: The WHO recommends fixed-dose combination products to improve patient satisfaction and reduce prescription errors or drug resistance. These combinations simplify treatment regimens, enhance programmatic management, and ensure consistent drug intake.
keyPoints: WHO recommends fixed-dose combination products over separate formulations for TB treatment.
Fixed-dose combinations include isoniazid/rifampin, isoniazid/rifampin/pyrazinamide, and isoniazid/rifampin/pyrazinamide/ethambutol.
These combinations increase patient satisfaction and minimize prescription errors or monotherapy-related drug resistance.
Fixed-dose products facilitate programmatic management of procurement and supply.
Quality assurance for fixed-dose combinations is crucial, especially in limited-resource countries.

items.subtopic: MONITORING TREATMENT RESPONSE AND DRUG TOXICITY
items.title: Monitoring Sputum Conversion in Pulmonary TB Treatment
items.content: Regular monitoring of sputum cultures is crucial for assessing the response to TB treatment. The majority of drug-susceptible patients will have negative sputum cultures by the end of the second month, and virtually all should be culture-negative by the third month.
keyPoints: >80% of drug-susceptible TB patients will have negative sputum cultures at the end of the second month
By the end of the third month, sputum of virtually all patients should be culture negative
Sputum examination is essential monthly for pulmonary disease until cultures are negative
items.reference: Tuberculosis · MONITORING TREATMENT RESPONSE AND DRUG TOXICITY

items.subtopic: MONITORING TREATMENT RESPONSE AND DRUG TOXICITY
items.title: Identifying and Managing Drug Toxicity in TB Treatment
items.content: Monitoring for drug toxicity, especially hepatitis, is important during TB treatment. Patients should be educated on symptoms of drug-induced hepatitis and undergo baseline liver function tests.
keyPoints: Hepatitis is the most common significant adverse reaction
Baseline assessment of liver function is recommended for all adult patients
Suspension of treatment considered with symptomatic hepatitis or marked elevations in AST/ALT levels
items.reference: Tuberculosis · MONITORING TREATMENT RESPONSE AND DRUG TOXICITY

items.subtopic: MONITORING TREATMENT RESPONSE AND DRUG TOXICITY
items.title: Approach to Persistent Positive Sputum Cultures in TB Treatment
items.content: Persistent positive sputum cultures at ≥3 months despite good adherence suggest treatment failure due to drug resistance, necessitating immediate testing or retesting for drug resistance.
keyPoints: Patients with cavitary disease and no culture conversion by 2 months require immediate drug resistance testing
Treatment failure likely if sputum cultures/smears remain positive at ≥3 months despite adherence
items.reference: Tuberculosis · MONITORING TREATMENT RESPONSE AND DRUG TOXICITY

items.title: Diagnosing Treatment Failure in Tuberculosis
items.content: Treatment failure should be suspected if a patient's cultures (or sputum smears) remain positive after 3 months of treatment. Urgent retesting for susceptibility to first-line agents is crucial, especially testing for rifampin and isoniazid resistance.
keyPoints: Suspect treatment failure if cultures are positive after 3 months of treatment.
Urgently retest current isolate for susceptibility to first-line agents.
Molecular testing should include at least rifampin and isoniazid resistance.
If clinical condition deteriorates rapidly, change regimen earlier by adding two or three drugs.

items.title: Managing Relapse in Tuberculosis
items.content: Relapse after successful treatment is less likely to involve drug-resistant strains. Treatment decisions are based on the risk of resistance, case severity, and rapid susceptibility testing results.
keyPoints: Relapses are less likely to harbor drug-resistant strains than treatment failures.
Acquired resistance is uncommon in relapses following a standard 6-month regimen.
Assess risk of drug resistance, case severity, and DST results for treatment decisions.
Patients with interrupted treatment and high MDR-TB likelihood should receive an MDR-TB regimen promptly.

items.title: Mechanisms and Mutation Rates Leading to Drug Resistance
items.content: Drug-resistant strains of M. tuberculosis arise due to spontaneous point mutations in the mycobacterial genome, with predictable mutation rates for key drugs.
keyPoints: Mutation rates: $10^{-7}-10^{-10}$ for key drugs.
Rifampin resistance: 95% associated with rpoB gene mutations.
Isoniazid resistance: Mainly katG gene (50–95%) and inhA promoter region (up to 45%).
Pyrazinamide resistance: pncA gene (up to 98%).
Ethambutol resistance: embB gene (50–65%).
Fluoroquinolones resistance: gyrA-gyrB genes (75–95%).
Aminoglycosides resistance: rrs gene (up to 80%), with C-12T mutation common in Eastern Europe.

items.title: Prevention and Management of Drug-Resistant TB
items.content: Effective prevention and management strategies for drug-resistant TB include adherence to treatment principles, early detection, and respiratory infection-control measures.
keyPoints: Drug resistance development: Often follows monotherapy or improper patient adherence.
Primary vs. acquired resistance: Primary from initial infection; acquired during treatment.
Prevention: Use at least two bactericidal drugs, effective regimens, supervised treatment, and fixed-dose combinations.
Transmission prevention: Implement respiratory control measures and early detection with immediate treatment.

items.title: Treatment Regimen for Isoniazid-Resistant TB
items.content: For isoniazid-resistant but rifampin-susceptible TB, a specific treatment regimen is recommended to ensure effectiveness and avoid MDR-TB.
keyPoints: Recommended regimen: Rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide, and levofloxacin for 6 months.
Rifampin resistance must be excluded before using fluoroquinolone-containing regimens.
Alternative regimen if fluoroquinolones are contraindicated: Rifampin, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide for 6 months.
Isoniazid may be retained in treatment to facilitate use of fixed-dose formulations.

items.title: WHO's Preferred Regimens for MDR/RR-TB
items.content: The WHO recommends four approaches to treat MDR/RR-TB, prioritizing all-oral regimens. These include a 6-month regimen with bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid (600 mg), and moxifloxacin; another 6-month regimen with bedaquiline, delamanid, linezolid (600 mg), levofloxacin, and clofazimine; various 9-month oral regimens containing bedaquiline; and an individualized longer regimen. The choice between shorter or longer regimens depends on disease severity, drug resistance patterns, and prior treatment history.
keyPoints: WHO recommends four approaches for MDR/RR-TB: (1) a 6-month all-oral regimen with bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid (600 mg), and moxifloxacin; (2) a 6-month oral regimen with bedaquiline, delamanid, linezolid (600 mg), levofloxacin, and clofazimine; (3) various 9-month oral regimens containing bedaquiline; (4) an individualized longer regimen of at least 18 months.
All-oral regimens are preferred for their ease of administration.
Regimen choice depends on disease severity, drug resistance pattern, and previous treatment history.

items.title: BPaLM Regimen for MDR/RR-TB
items.content: The BPaLM regimen, consisting of bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid (600 mg), and moxifloxacin, is recommended by WHO due to its high treatment success rate (~90%) and fewer adverse events compared to previous trials. It's suitable for patients aged 14 or older with MDR/RR-TB or pre-XDR-TB, excluding those with disseminated, central nervous system, or osteoarticular TB, and is not recommended for pregnant or breastfeeding women.
keyPoints: WHO recommends the BPaLM regimen (bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid 600 mg, moxifloxacin) due to ~90% treatment success rate.
Adverse events are fewer with a daily dose of linezolid at 600 mg compared to 1200 mg.
Suitable for patients aged 14 or older with MDR/RR-TB or pre-XDR-TB, excluding certain TB forms and pregnant/breastfeeding women.

items.title: BDLLfxCfz Regimen for MDR/RR-TB
items.content: The BDLLfxCfz regimen, a 6-month all-oral treatment with bedaquiline, delamanid, linezolid (600 mg), levofloxacin, and clofazimine, is noninferior to longer regimens. It can be adjusted based on fluoroquinolone susceptibility testing: omit clofazimine if susceptible or levofloxacin if resistant. This regimen is suitable for patients with no prior exposure (or less than 1 month) to its components and can be used in children, adolescents, and pregnant/breastfeeding women.
keyPoints: BDLLfxCfz regimen is noninferior to longer regimens and consists of bedaquiline, delamanid, linezolid (600 mg), levofloxacin, and clofazimine.
Adjustment based on fluoroquinolone susceptibility: omit clofazimine if susceptible or levofloxacin if resistant.
Suitable for patients with no prior exposure to its components and can be used in children, adolescents, and pregnant/breastfeeding women.

items.title: 9-Month All-Oral MDR-TB Regimens
items.content: For MDR/RR-TB patients without resistance to fluoroquinolones and limited exposure to bedaquiline, delamanid, and linezolid, a 9-month all-oral regimen containing bedaquiline is non-inferior to the traditional 18-month regimens. These regimens include combinations such as BLMZ or BLLfxCfzZ.
keyPoints: Non-inferiority of 9-month regimens compared to 18-month ones for specific patient groups
Regimen options: (i) bedaquiline, linezolid, moxifloxacin, and pyrazinamide (BLMZ); (ii) bedaquiline, linezolid, levofloxacin, clofazimine, and pyrazinamide (BLLfxCfzZ)
Suitable for patients with no or <1 month exposure to second-line drugs
DST essential before starting the 9-month regimen

items.title: Longer MDR-TB Regimens and Drug Grouping
items.content: For some MDR/RR-TB patients, longer regimens are recommended. These should include at least three group A agents and one group B agent to ensure effectiveness. Levofloxacin or moxifloxacin, bedaquiline, and linezolid must be included.
keyPoints: Longer regimen duration: 18–20 months, with adjustments based on patient response
Intensive course of 6–7 months is recommended for most patients
Group A agents plus at least one group B agent are essential; clofazimine and cycloserine (or terizidone) as options
Injectable agents like kanamycin and capreomycin should be avoided due to higher failure risks

items.title: Safety Monitoring in MDR-TB Treatment
items.content: Informed consent is crucial for all MDR-TB regimens. QT interval-prolonging drugs require electrocardiography monitoring, and patients on amikacin should have serial audiometry to detect hearing loss.
keyPoints: Informed consent required for all MDR-TB treatments
QT interval-prolonging drugs need ECG monitoring; avoid in patients with QTc >500 ms or history of ventricular arrhythmias
Serial audiometry recommended for patients on amikacin to monitor hearing loss

items.title: Management of MDR-TB: Early Intervention and Support
items.content: Aggressive management of multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB), including early drug susceptibility testing, rational drug combinations, directly observed therapy, monthly bacteriologic monitoring, and intensive patient support, can increase cure rates and reduce mortality.
keyPoints: Early drug susceptibility testing is crucial for effective MDR-TB management.
Directly observed therapy and monthly bacteriologic monitoring are recommended.
Intensive patient support may interrupt transmission and avert death.

items.title: ART Initiation in HIV-associated TB: Timing and Benefits
items.content: Initiating antiretroviral therapy (ART) early during anti-TB treatment significantly reduces mortality rates, especially for patients with CD4+ T cell counts of <50/μL. ART should ideally start within the first 2 weeks of anti-TB therapy.
keyPoints: Early ART initiation can reduce mortality by 34–68%.
ART is recommended for all HIV-infected TB patients, regardless of CD4+ T cell count.
For CNS-involved TB, delay ART initiation for the first 8 weeks.

items.title: Considerations for Rifamycin and ART Interactions in PLWH with TB
items.content: Rifampin, used in TB treatment, can lower serum levels of many ART drugs due to enzyme induction. Adjustments include using integrase inhibitors like dolutegravir or raltegravir at double doses, or efavirenz as an alternative.
keyPoints: Rifampin induces cytochrome P450 enzymes affecting HIV protease and nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors.
Use integrase inhibitors (dolutegravir or raltegravir) at double doses with rifampin-based TB regimens.
Efavirenz can be used as an alternative to integrase inhibitors.

items.title: Treatment Duration for Special Forms of TB
items.content: The treatment duration for extrapulmonary TB generally follows a 6-month regimen similar to pulmonary TB, with specific extensions for certain conditions. For TB meningitis, the continuation phase is extended to 7–10 months as recommended by ATS, CDC, and IDSA. The WHO and American Academy of Pediatrics suggest up to 12 months for children with bone/joint TB, tuberculous meningitis, or miliary TB.
keyPoints: 6-month regimen for most extrapulmonary TB
7–10 months continuation phase for TB meningitis (ATS, CDC, IDSA recommendation)
Up to 12 months treatment for children with bone/joint TB, tuberculous meningitis, or miliary TB (WHO and American Academy of Pediatrics recommendation)

items.title: TB Treatment Considerations in Special Populations
items.content: Special considerations are necessary when treating TB in patients with underlying medical conditions. For those with chronic renal failure, aminoglycosides should be avoided and ethambutol used only if serum levels can be monitored; isoniazid, rifampin, and pyrazinamide may be given in usual doses for mild to moderate renal failure but require dosage modification of pyrazinamide. In patients with hepatic disease, avoid hepatotoxic drugs like isoniazid, rifampin, and pyrazinamide unless under close supervision; ethambutol, streptomycin, or fluoroquinolones may be used instead.
keyPoints: Avoid aminoglycosides in chronic renal failure
Ethambutol only if serum levels can be monitored in renal failure
Usual doses of isoniazid, rifampin, and pyrazinamide for mild to moderate renal failure; modify pyrazinamide dosage
Avoid hepatotoxic drugs (isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide) in severe hepatic disease unless under supervision
Use ethambutol, streptomycin, or fluoroquinolones for severe hepatic disease

items.title: BCG Vaccination Efficacy and Safety Profile
items.content: The Bacille Calmette-Guérin (BCG) vaccine, derived from an attenuated strain of M. bovis, varies in efficacy from 80% to nil based on randomized trials. It is particularly effective against serious disseminated forms of childhood TB like tuberculous meningitis and miliary TB.
keyPoints: Efficacy ranges from 80% to nil in randomized, placebo-controlled trials.
Higher efficacy observed in protecting infants and young children from severe TB forms.
BCG is safe but can cause complications such as ulceration at the vaccination site (1-10%) and osteomyelitis (~1 case per million).
Disseminated BCG infection occurs in 1–10 cases per 10 million doses, mostly in immunocompromised individuals.

items.title: BCG Vaccination Recommendations and Limitations
items.content: BCG is recommended at birth in high TB prevalence areas but not for general use in low-risk countries like the U.S. due to its variable protection and impact on TST results.
keyPoints: Recommended for routine use at birth in high TB prevalence regions.
Not recommended for general use in low-risk, high-income countries.
HIV-infected individuals and those with unknown HIV status but risk factors should not receive BCG.

items.title: Advancements in TB Vaccine Development
items.content: Research efforts have led to new vaccine candidates, such as M72/AS01E, which showed about 50% efficacy at 36 months in a phase 2b trial. As of the end of 2024, 15 candidate vaccines were in various stages of clinical trials.
keyPoints: M72/AS01E vaccine demonstrated ~50% efficacy at 36 months in a phase 2b trial.
Adverse events similar between vaccine and placebo groups.
15 TB vaccine candidates in clinical trials as of end of 2024, including live attenuated, viral vector, recombinant protein, and RNA vaccines.

items.title: Importance of TB Preventive Treatment in Control Strategies
items.content: TB preventive treatment (TPT), also known as chemoprophylaxis or preventive chemotherapy, is crucial for controlling and eliminating tuberculosis. Despite only a small fraction of infections progressing to active disease, new cases continue to emerge from the pool of infected individuals.
keyPoints: Nearly 2 billion individuals have been infected with M. tuberculosis in their lifetime.
TPT is fundamental in TB control and elimination strategies.

items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Testing Methods for TB Infection
items.content: Current tests like TST, TBSTs, or IGRA measure host immune response to TB antigens but do not confirm true infection or predict progression to active TB. The Mantoux method is used for skin testing with a specific reading protocol.
keyPoints: No gold-standard diagnostic test confirms true infection or predicts progression to active TB.
TST involves injecting five tuberculosis units of polysorbate-stabilized PPD intradermally and reading reactions at 48–72 hours as the diameter of induration.

items.title: Criteria for Positive TB Test Results and Prophylactic Treatment
items.content: Positive test results depend on risk groups, with different cutoffs for induration diameter. High-risk individuals should receive systematic testing and TPT, while decisions for low-risk persons are based on individual risk/benefit considerations.
keyPoints: Induration $ \geq $5 mm is positive for high-risk groups like PLWH and those with silicosis or end-stage renal disease.
A 10-mm cutoff defines positive reactions in most other at-risk individuals, while a 15-mm cutoff applies to very low-risk persons.
WHO recommends systematic testing and TPT for high-risk groups such as HIV-positive individuals, household contacts of TB patients, and those with certain medical conditions.

items.title: Indications for TB Preventive Treatment in High-Risk Populations
items.content: Infants and children under 5 years exposed to infectious TB cases, as well as PLWH over 1 year of age who have been exposed to an infectious TB patient, should receive TPT regardless of TB infection test results. This is crucial for preventing active TB in these high-risk groups.
keyPoints: Infants and children <5 years exposed to TB cases should receive TPT even without a positive TB test.
PLWH >1 year exposed to infectious TB patients should receive TPT regardless of TB infection test results.
Screening for active TB is essential before starting TPT in HIV-infected individuals.

items.title: Clinical Algorithm for Starting TPT in PLWH
items.content: A clinical algorithm based on four symptoms—current cough, fever, weight loss, and night sweats—is used to determine if PLWH can start TPT. The absence of all four symptoms tends to exclude active TB, while the presence of any symptom warrants further investigation.
keyPoints: Clinical algorithm includes current cough, fever, weight loss, and night sweats.
Absence of all four symptoms generally excludes active TB in PLWH.
Presence of one or more symptoms requires further investigation for active TB before starting TPT.

items.title: Effective Regimens and Duration for TB Preventive Treatment
items.content: A 6- to 9-month course of isoniazid reduces the risk of active TB by up to 90%. A shorter regimen of isoniazid (900 mg) and rifapentine (900 mg) given weekly for 12 weeks has shown similar efficacy with higher treatment completion rates and less hepatotoxicity.
keyPoints: 6-9 months of isoniazid reduces active TB risk by up to 90%.
Isoniazid-rifapentine regimen: 900 mg each, weekly for 12 weeks.
Higher completion rate (82%) and less hepatotoxicity (0.4%) with isoniazid-rifapentine compared to isoniazid alone.

items.title: WHO Recommendation for Shortened TPT Regimen
items.content: The WHO now includes a 1-month regimen of daily isoniazid and rifapentine as an option for PLWH aged 13 years or older, based on noninferiority to the traditional 9-month isoniazid regimen.
keyPoints: A 1-month regimen of daily isoniazid (300 mg) plus rifapentine (600 mg) is recommended by WHO for patients aged 13 and above.
This regimen ensures higher treatment completion compared to a 9-month isoniazid regimen.

items.title: Considerations for Rifamycin Use in PLWH
items.content: Rifampin and rifapentine are contraindicated with certain antiretrovirals due to drug interactions, but some combinations like efavirenz or tenofovir disoproxil do not require dose adjustments.
keyPoints: Rifampin and rifapentine should be avoided in PLWH on protease inhibitors or most nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (e.g., nevirapine).
Dolutegravir requires a dose increase to 50 mg twice daily when administered with rifampin.
Efavirenz and tenofovir disoproxil can be used without dose adjustments alongside a rifamycin.

items.title: Approach to TPT in MDR-TB Exposure
items.content: In the absence of clinical trial data, individualized risk assessment and drug susceptibility profiles guide TPT decisions for those exposed to MDR-TB.
keyPoints: TPT among persons likely infected by a multidrug-resistant strain is challenging due to lack of guiding trial results.
WHO recommends daily levofloxacin for 6 months in people exposed to MDR-TB.
Drug selection should be based on the drug susceptibility profile of the index case.

items.title: Early Detection and Treatment in TB Control
items.content: The cornerstone of effective tuberculosis (TB) control is the early detection of infectious cases, followed by treatment under optimal case-management conditions with social support until cure. Regular screening of high-risk groups such as immigrants from high-prevalence countries, migrant workers, prisoners, homeless individuals, substance abusers, and HIV-seropositive persons is crucial for prompt detection and treatment.
keyPoints: Early detection and treatment are priorities in TB control.
Regular screening of high-risk groups is recommended.
Social support until cure is essential.

items.title: Multisectoral Approach to TB Elimination
items.content: The WHO's 'End TB' strategy emphasizes a multisectoral and holistic approach, integrating patient-centered care, bold policies, supportive systems, and intensified research. Beyond health sector interventions, addressing direct risk factors like HIV infection, smoking, alcohol abuse, diabetes, and socioeconomic determinants such as poverty and inadequate living conditions is essential for TB elimination.
keyPoints: 'End TB' strategy relies on three pillars: integrated care, bold policies, and research.
A fourth multisectoral pillar addresses risk factors and socioeconomic determinants.
Elimination requires control of direct risk factors and socioeconomic determinants.

items.question: Which organism in the M. tuberculosis complex is most commonly associated with human disease?
items.explanation: M. tuberculosis (sensu stricto) is the most common and important agent of human disease in the M. tuberculosis complex.

items.question: What characteristic justifies the classification of Mycobacterium tuberculosis as acid-fast bacilli?
options: They cannot be decolorized by acid alcohol once stained.
They have a high guanine-plus-cytosine content.
items.explanation: Mycobacterium tuberculosis is classified as acid-fast bacilli because they cannot be decolorized by acid alcohol once stained.

items.question: Which member of the M. tuberculosis complex is characteristically resistant to pyrazinamide?
items.explanation: M. bovis is characteristically resistant to pyrazinamide.

items.question: What is the main reason for the low permeability of the Mycobacterium tuberculosis cell wall?
options: High content of mycolic acids and other lipids.
items.explanation: The high content of mycolic acids and other cell-wall lipids results in very low permeability of the Mycobacterium tuberculosis cell wall.

items.question: What percentage of the estimated new TB cases worldwide in 2023 were reported to WHO?
items.explanation: Reported cases represent only about two-thirds (approximately 67%) of the total estimated cases.

items.question: Which country accounted for the highest percentage of new TB cases in 2023?
items.explanation: India accounted for 26% of all new TB cases, which was the highest among listed countries.

items.question: In 2023, what percentage of TB deaths occurred in low- and middle-income countries?
items.explanation: 98% of the estimated 1.25 million TB-related deaths occurred in low- and middle-income countries.

items.question: Which population group had the highest incidence rate per capita for TB in the United States in 2021?
options: Non-U.S.-born persons of more than one ethnicity
items.explanation: The highest rates per capita were among non-U.S.-born persons of more than one ethnicity (30 cases per 100,000 population).

items.question: Which route is most commonly responsible for the transmission of M. tuberculosis?
options: Droplet nuclei from coughing, sneezing, or speaking
items.explanation: M. tuberculosis is most commonly transmitted by droplet nuclei containing M. tuberculosis bacteria.

items.question: Which patients are considered the most infectious for transmitting TB?
options: Patients whose sputum contains AFB visible by microscopy and have cavitary pulmonary disease
items.explanation: The most infectious patients are those whose sputum contains AFB visible by microscopy and have cavitary pulmonary disease.

items.question: What environmental condition significantly increases the transmission of tubercle bacilli?
items.explanation: Crowding in poorly ventilated rooms significantly increases the transmission of tubercle bacilli.

items.question: What is the classification of clinical illness that occurs directly following M. tuberculosis infection?
items.explanation: Clinical illness directly following infection with M. tuberculosis is classified as primary TB.

items.question: Which factor is the most potent risk for developing active TB among infected individuals?
items.explanation: The most potent risk factor for developing active TB among infected individuals is HIV co-infection.

items.question: What percentage of infected persons are estimated to eventually develop active TB in their lifetime?
items.explanation: It is estimated that up to 10% of infected persons will eventually develop active TB in their lifetime.
